name: Agua Viva Route
name_original: El Castillo de Almansa hasta el Embalse de Almansa
description: Jog the difficult 9.9-mile Agua Viva Route in Almansa, Spain, featuring historical sites and reservoir views with 557 feet of elevation gain
region_intro: Jogging around Almansa offers a diverse landscape for runners, characterized by its mountainous terrain, fertile plains, and historical elements. The region features natural landmarks such as the Sierra de Almansa and the Almansa Reservoir, providing varied backdrops for running. Trails often incorporate views of the historic Castle of Almansa and sections of the Camino de Santiago, blending natural exploration with cultural sights. This mix of elevated areas and flatter paths caters to different preferences and fitness levels. Best jogging routes around Almansa The most popular jogging route is Almansa and Cueva del Quemao Trail, a 5.1 miles (8.1 km) trail that takes 52 minutes to complete. This moderate route combines natural exploration with cultural landmarks like the Church of the Assumption. Another top favourite among local runners is Rambla de los Molinos and Cuco Garganchín Loop, a difficult 11.1 miles (17.9 km) path. This route provides extensive options for exploring the local environment. Local runners also love the Ruta Agua Viva — circular in Almansa, a 3.0 miles (4.9 km) trail leading through varied terrain near the Almansa Reservoir, often completed in about 33 minutes. Jogging around Almansa is defined by mountainous terrain, historic reservoirs, and sections of the Camino de Santiago. The network of running routes offers options for different ability levels, from easy loops to more challenging trails with significant elevation gain. The routes in Almansa are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 3.0 stars from over 6 reviews. More than 280 runners have used komoot to explore Almansa's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many running routes are available around Almansa?
A: There are over 60 dedicated running routes around Almansa, offering a wide range of options for different preferences and fitness levels. The komoot community has explored more than 280 of these trails.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on jogging trails in Almansa?
A: Almansa's jogging routes feature a diverse landscape, from the mountainous terrain of the Sierra de Almansa to fertile plains. You'll find paths that offer uphill challenges and panoramic vistas, as well as flatter sections suitable for easier runs. Many routes also incorporate historical elements and sections of the Camino de Santiago.

Q: Are there any easy running routes for beginners in Almansa?
A: Yes, Almansa offers several easy running routes. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1081119642/ruta-agua-viva-circular-in-almansa" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ruta Agua Viva — circular in Almansa&lt;/a&gt; is an easy 3.0-mile (4.9 km) trail that leads through varied terrain near the Almansa Reservoir, typically completed in about 33 minutes.

Q: What natural features or landmarks can I see while jogging in Almansa?
A: Jogging in Almansa allows you to experience a blend of natural beauty and historical sites. You can run past the impressive &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5287878" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castle of Almansa&lt;/a&gt;, explore trails around the historic Almansa Reservoir, and enjoy views from the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4713383" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Viewpoint of the Museum Trail&lt;/a&gt;. Some routes also pass by the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5674312" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Church of the Assumption&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any circular running routes in the Almansa area?
A: Yes, Almansa has several circular running routes. A notable one is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1081119932/almansa-and-cueva-del-quemao-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Almansa and Cueva del Quemao Trail&lt;/a&gt;, a moderate 5.1-mile (8.1 km) circular route that combines natural exploration with cultural landmarks.

Q: What do other runners say about the trails in Almansa?
A: The running routes in Almansa are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 3.0 stars from over 6 reviews. Runners often praise the diverse terrain and the opportunity to combine outdoor activity with historical sights.

Q: Are there any challenging trail running options in Almansa?
A: For those seeking a challenge, Almansa offers several difficult routes. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1081126097/rambla-de-los-molinos-and-cuco-garganchin-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Rambla de los Molinos and Cuco Garganchín Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 11.1-mile (17.9 km) path with significant elevation changes, providing extensive options for exploring the local environment.

Q: Can I find routes that are suitable for families or those with children?
A: While specific family-friendly routes are not detailed, the presence of easy loops and paths around the Almansa Reservoir suggests options for families. The 3.1-mile loop around the Castle of Almansa, which is mostly paved, could also be suitable for a family jog.

Q: Are there any running routes that incorporate sections of the Camino de Santiago?
A: Yes, Almansa is a junction for two pilgrim routes of the Camino de Santiago – the Camino de la Lana and the Camino de Levante. Many local jogging routes incorporate sections of these extensive paths, offering a unique blend of running and historical exploration.

Q: What is the best time of year to go running in Almansa?
A: Almansa's climate generally allows for running throughout much of the year. However, spring and autumn typically offer the most pleasant temperatures for outdoor activities, avoiding the peak heat of summer and potential chill of winter.

Q: Are there any viewpoints along the running trails in Almansa?
A: Yes, several trails offer scenic viewpoints. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4713383" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Viewpoint of the Museum Trail&lt;/a&gt; near the Almansa Reservoir is a notable spot for picturesque views during a jog. Routes in the Sierra de Almansa and Sierra de la Oliva also provide opportunities for panoramic vistas.
tips:
The Palace of the Counts of Cirat, also known as “the Big House”, is a Renaissance-style building with a civil character. It was built in the last quarter of the 16th century by the Almanseño noble Don Alfonso de Pina. This palace is located in the Plaza de Santa María in the town of Almansa.
This palace is a historical and architectural testimony that deserves to be appreciated for its beauty and cultural significance. If you ever have the opportunity to visit Almansa, I recommend exploring this magnificent Renaissance building. 🏰🌟

The Archpriestal Church of the Assumption in Almansa is an impressive historical and architectural monument. Located in the Plaza de Santa María, next to the Palace of the Counts of Cirat, this church was built in several stages from the 16th to the 19th century.

Currently the swamp is part of the "Agua Viva" project, and serves as a resting place for migratory waterfowl.
From this viewpoint you can see a large part of the swamp.
It has benches to sit on and an explanatory panel.

The floating walkway of the Almansa Reservoir, also known as the Pantalán del Dique, is a spectacular pedestrian platform measuring 64 metres in length. Inaugurated in April 2015 and renovated a year later, this walkway is located on an artificial water containment dam.

The original construction could have been the work of the Almohads, who used to use the rammed earth technique for their defensive buildings.
The remains of rammed earth walls in this castle, traditionally attributed to the Almohad period, were actually built during the first decades of Christian rule, at the end of the 13th century or the beginning of the 14th century.
In the 14th century, Almansa and its fortress were incorporated into the Villena manor. Don Juan Manuel took advantage of the previous construction and ordered its walls to be rebuilt.

faqs.question: What is the starting point for the Agua Viva Route?
faqs.answer: The Agua Viva Route typically starts near the Castle of Almansa, leading towards the Embalse de Almansa (Almansa Reservoir). This makes it convenient to begin your jogging adventure from a prominent local landmark.

faqs.question: Is the Agua Viva Route suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: Given its 'difficult' grading, the Agua Viva Route is generally more suited for experienced joggers or those with a good level of fitness. While it's a jogging route, the difficulty suggests it might present challenges for beginners.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on this route?
faqs.answer: While specific terrain details aren't provided, as a jogging route graded 'difficult' and connecting a castle to a reservoir, you can anticipate varied surfaces. It's wise to be prepared for uneven paths, potentially some inclines, and possibly a mix of natural and paved sections, especially near the reservoir and historical sites.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the Agua Viva Route?
faqs.answer: Along the route, you'll encounter several notable highlights. These include the historic Palace of the Counts of Cirat, the impressive Church of the Assumption (Iglesia de la Asunción), and the scenic Viewpoint of the Museum Trail. A unique feature is the Floating Walkway of the Almansa Reservoir, and of course, the iconic Castle of Almansa.

faqs.question: Does the route intersect with other trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route incorporates sections of several official trails. You'll find yourself on parts of the Camino de Santiago de Levante, Camino del Alba, Camino de San José, and the Vereda de Madrid a Alicante. It also extensively uses the Ruta Agua Viva and passes through Calle Morería.

faqs.question: Is the Agua Viva Route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: Generally, jogging routes in this region are dog-friendly, but it's always best to keep your dog on a leash, especially when passing through populated areas or near historical sites. Be sure to carry water for your pet and clean up after them.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to jog this route?
faqs.answer: For jogging, spring and autumn typically offer the most pleasant temperatures in this region, avoiding the intense heat of summer and the cooler, potentially wetter conditions of winter. Early mornings or late afternoons are ideal during warmer months.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or pay an entrance fee for the Agua Viva Route?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to jog the Agua Viva Route itself, as it utilizes public paths and roads. However, if you plan to enter any of the historical sites like the Castle of Almansa or the Palace of the Counts of Cirat, there might be separate admission fees.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the Agua Viva Route?
faqs.answer: Since the route starts near the Castle of Almansa, you should look for parking options in the vicinity of the castle or within the town of Almansa. Public parking areas are usually available, though they might be limited during peak times.

faqs.question: Are there any facilities like cafes or restrooms along the route?
faqs.answer: As the route passes through and near the town of Almansa, you'll find various cafes, restaurants, and public restrooms available, especially closer to the starting point at the castle and within the town center. It's advisable to use facilities before heading out on longer stretches towards the reservoir.

faqs.question: What should I bring for jogging the Agua Viva Route?
faqs.answer: Given the 'difficult' grading and the length, it's recommended to bring adequate water, especially during warmer months. Comfortable jogging shoes suitable for varied terrain, sun protection (hat, sunscreen), and a fully charged phone for navigation or emergencies are also essential. Consider light snacks if you plan a longer run.

Church of the Assumption (Iglesia de la Asunción), Almansa
